comment_text.sn1.13:2.2: Vājasaneyi Saṁhitā 23.48b says cattle have no measure.
comment_text.sn1.13:2.3: These lines closely mirror the question at Vājasaneyi Saṁhitā 23.47a (quoted at Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 13.5.2.13), “What light equals the sun? What lake equals the ocean?” The answers given in the next verse are “Brahmā” and “heaven”.
comment_text.sn1.13:3.1: The Theravada tradition, starting from the Vimuttimagga, begins the meditation on love (mettā) by focusing on oneself, a practice that draws on passages such as this. See also SN 3.8, Ud 5.1. Here the Pali uses pema (“fondness”) rather than mettā (“love”).

root_text.sn1.13:1.2: Ekamantaṁ ṭhitā kho sā devatā bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:

translation_text.sn1.13:1.2: Standing to one side, that deity spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence:
translation_text.sn1.13:2.1: “There’s no fondness like that for a child,
translation_text.sn1.13:2.3: no light like that of the sun,
translation_text.sn1.13:2.4: and of lakes the ocean is paramount.”
translation_text.sn1.13:3.1: “There’s no fondness like that for oneself,
translation_text.sn1.13:3.3: no light like that of wisdom,
translation_text.sn1.13:3.4: and of waters the rain is paramount.”

variant_text.sn1.13:2.3: sūriyasamā → suriyasamā (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed)
